This paragraph has real ambition and mostly earns it. "It moves where the scarce thing lives" is a genuinely good sentence — specific, defensible, and quotable. The bottleneck-shifting argument ("stops being production and becomes judgment, prioritization, review") is a real claim that a real person could disagree with, which is the gold standard. The final sentence about "redesigned the operating model" is doing actual intellectual work, not just vibing.

That said, it doesn't fully escape AI-flavored abstraction. "The companies that win the next decade" is a classic framing tic — smooth, forward-looking, stakes-sounding, but with no skin in the game. It could appear in any McKinsey deck or GPT-written LinkedIn post. The contrasting structure ("not the ones that bought the most seats… but the ones that redesigned") is clean but pattern-matchable; it's the rhetorical skeleton of a hundred thought-leadership pieces. And "trusted work instead of expensive noise" is a snappy close that sounds good but doesn't actually define either term. The paragraph is readable and has a point of view, but it's one editorial pass away from being fully concrete.

The closest relevant entry (claims#40) supports hiring for judgment and taste, but does not speak to the breakdown of traditional competence signals in interviews.

Neither of these named-person attributions is catastrophically central to the chapter's thesis, but they are substantive claims with no ledger backing. The compensation restructuring claim is a moderate-weight claim (not the paragraph's most central point), and the hiring-signal claim is also moderate in weight. Together they constitute two unsupported claims, one of which (the compensation model) is somewhat peripheral and one of which (interview signal degradation) is moderately central to the paragraph's second half. This is a minor rhetorical excess on a supporting claim.
